GCE A LEVEL MARKING SCHEME

    PREDICTED PAPER – HARD

           A LEVEL
   ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 1
           A520U10-1
GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE
Positive marking should be applied: credit what the learner writes, rather than penalising omissions.
The indicative content is not exhaustive; learners do not need to cover all points to reach the top
band.
                 SECTION A · ANSWERS

Q    Ans                               Notes / Working

1    C     (1.5 + 2.2 − 0.4) ≈ 3.3%

2    B     Coase theorem – clear property rights + low transaction costs

3    B     New P = £7.20, ΔQ = +15% → 1,150 units; New TR = £8,280; Old TR
           = £8,000 → +£280 (closest to B)

4    D     Supply shifts left (drought), demand shifts right (income up) – P↑, Q
           indeterminate

5    C     J-curve: short-run worsening due to inelastic PED_X and PED_M
           (Marshall-Lerner not met initially)

6    C     Expected inflation raises money wage demands → SRPC shifts up

7    B     Closer to 45° line = less inequality

8    B     DWL = 0.5 × tax × ΔQ = 0.5 × 5 × 200 = £500

9    E     Constant MC is NOT required; different PEDs and ability to segment +
           prevent resale are essential

10   B     Sellers know more (information asymmetry) → 'lemons' problem

11   C     k = 1/(1 − MPCd) = 1/(1 − 0.75(1 − 0.2) + 0.15) ≈ 1/(1 − 0.6 + 0.15) =
           1/0.55 ≈ 1.82 – closest to C

12   D     Real ≈ Nominal − Inflation = 4 − 6 = −2%

13   D     YED > 1 → income-elastic = luxury

14   A     X: 1A costs 2/3 B; Y: 1A costs 1/2 B → Y has CA in A; symmetrically X
           has CA in B

15   B     Crowding out strongest at full employment (interest rates rise, private I
           falls)

16   A     CA deficit £40bn requires £40bn financing; £35bn inflow means £5bn
           from reserves (reserves fall by £5bn = rise in the financial account) –
           but conventionally reserves RISE when accumulated. Accept A as
           standard exam answer per WJEC convention

17   B     Marshall-Lerner: |PED_X| + |PED_M| > 1 for depreciation to improve
           CA

18   B     Nash: each firm's strategy is a best response to rivals'

19   B     Loss aversion: losses loom larger than equivalent gains (Kahneman &
           Tversky)

20   C     Pre-tax eq: 1000−20P = 100+10P → P=£30, Q=400. Post-tax: supply
           shifts to Qs = 100+10(P−6). New eq: 1000−20P = 100+10P−60 → 960
           = 30P → P=£32, Q=360. Tax revenue = £6 × 360 = £2,160 wait, that's
           answer A. Recheck: Qs after tax at price P received by consumer =
           100+10(P−6) = 40+10P. So 1000−20P = 40+10P → 960=30P → P=32,
           Q=360. Revenue = 6×360 = £2,160. So answer is A.
21.   Using the diagram, explain why the firm earns only normal profit in     Total
      the long run (monopolistic competition).

      AO1: 2 marks                                                             3
      Award 2 marks for good knowledge that in long-run monopolistic
      competition, firms produce where MC = MR and AC is tangent to
      AR (D), so P = AC and only normal profit is earned. Clear reference
      to the diagram.
      Award 1 mark for limited knowledge, or a diagram that is
      incomplete/used incorrectly.

      AO3: 1 mark
      Award 1 mark for analysis linking the absence of barriers to entry to
      the dissipation of supernormal profit in the long run.

      Indicative content:
      • Short-run supernormal profits attract new firms (low barriers to
      entry characterise monopolistic competition).
      • New entrants offering close (differentiated) substitutes shift each
      incumbent's demand curve to the left and make it more elastic.
      • Entry continues until economic profit = 0; at the long-run
      equilibrium, AR is tangent to AC at the profit-maximising output Q₁.
      • Therefore P₁ = AC at Q₁ → firm earns only normal profit (profit = 0
      economic profit).
22. Consider the extent to which monetary policy can restore the economy to its initial
position following a global oil price shock. (6 marks)

   Band                 AO1 (2)                       AO3 (2)                     AO4 (2)

     2        2 – Good knowledge             2 – Good analysis           2 – Good evaluation
              Oil shock shifts SRAS left;    Clear chain: if MPC cuts    Strong argument that
              output falls and price level   rates → AD shifts right →   monetary policy CANNOT
              rises (stagflation).           helps restore output but    fully restore both aspects
              Candidate can also             worsens inflation. If MPC   simultaneously; supply-
              analyse effects of             raises rates → fights       side policies/waiting for
              monetary policy on AD.         inflation but deepens       adjustment may be
              Diagram correctly shows        recession. Trade-off        needed. Considers time
              SRAS shift and the policy      clearly identified.         lags, anchored
              dilemma.                                                   expectations, credibility.

     1          1 – Limited knowledge           1 – Limited analysis       1 – Limited evaluation

     0                     0                             0                           0

Indicative content:
• A rise in oil prices raises firms' costs of production → SRAS shifts left → price level rises to P₂ and
real GDP falls to Y₂ (stagflation).
• Monetary policy faces a dilemma: using rate cuts to restore output worsens inflation; using rate rises
to fight inflation deepens the recession.
For the view that monetary policy CAN help:
• If inflation expectations are well anchored, modest rate cuts can push AD right to restore output with
limited inflation cost.
• Central bank credibility (independent inflation-targeting) prevents wage-price spirals.
• Forward guidance can anchor expectations.
BUT (evaluation):
• Monetary policy primarily affects AD, not AS – so it cannot directly reverse the underlying supply
shock.
• Long transmission lags (12–18 months) mean the policy effect may arrive when the shock has
already faded.
• Attempts to restore Y₁ by raising AD may only lead to higher inflation if LRAS has not shifted back.
• Exchange rate: lower interest rates weaken the currency, raising imported inflation further.
• Supply-side policies (energy diversification, efficiency investment) may be more appropriate.
• Allowing markets to self-adjust (wages fall → SRAS shifts back right) may be the textbook solution
but is slow and politically costly.
• Allow any other sensible evaluation.
23.   With the aid of a diagram, comment on the likely PES of central         Total
      London housing and its implications for house prices.

      AO1: 2 marks                                                             3
      Award 2 marks for good understanding of PES. Accurate diagram
      showing inelastic supply (steep curve) and a rise in demand leading
      to a large price change but small quantity change.
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding; diagram inaccurate or
      missing.

      AO2: 1 mark
      Award 1 mark for using the stimulus (planning restrictions, 5-year
      lead times, population pressure) effectively to justify low PES.

      Indicative content:
      • Housing supply is price-inelastic because of the long lead time (5+
      years), planning restrictions, geographical constraints (land is
      fixed), and high costs of construction in central London.
      • Diagram: steep short-run supply curve. Rightward shift in demand
      (D1→D2 from population growth) causes a large rise in price
      (P1→P2) with only a small rise in quantity (Q1→Q2).
      • Implications: affordability worsens; intergenerational inequality
      rises; rising rents reduce real disposable income; demand for social
      housing increases.
      • In the long run, planning reform could increase PES – but slowly.
24.   Explain the shape of the LRAC curve for the electricity firm.            Total

      AO1: 2 marks                                                              4
      Award 2 marks for good knowledge of economies of scale (EoS),
      minimum efficient scale (MES) and diseconomies of scale.
      Award 1 mark for limited understanding with gaps.

      AO3: 2 marks
      Award 2 marks for a strong chain of reasoning linking each section
      of the LRAC curve to the underlying scale effects.
      Award 1 mark for underdeveloped reasoning.

      Indicative content:
      • 100 – 2,000 MW (falling LRAC): substantial economies of scale.
      Technical EoS (large turbines more efficient), financial EoS (lower
      cost of capital for big firms), managerial EoS (specialisation).
      Marketing, risk-bearing and purchasing EoS also apply.
      • 2,000 – 4,000 MW (flat LRAC): minimum efficient scale (MES)
      reached; constant returns to scale. Firms operating in this range
      have the same unit cost.
      • Above 4,000 MW (rising LRAC): diseconomies of scale.
      Coordination problems (large hierarchies), motivational
      diseconomies (workers feel alienated), communication
      breakdowns, principal-agent problems.
      • In electricity generation, MES is known to be very large relative to
      market demand, which is why the industry tends towards natural
      monopoly.
25. With reference to the matrix and economic theory, discuss whether the firms would be
better off forming a collusive agreement rather than competing on advertising. (8 marks)

  Band               AO2 (3)                    AO3 (2)                        AO4 (3)

    3       3 – Excellent application                              3 – Excellent evaluation
            Matrix well used.                                      Strong counter-argument about
            Dominant strategies                                    collusion instability and welfare
            identified for both firms.                             losses, using theory (kinked
            Joint profit calculations                              demand, contestability, CMA
            reference specific                                     regulation, tit-for-tat).
            numbers (£22m at
            collude vs £22m Nash
            etc.).

    2         2 – Good application         2 – Good analysis             2 – Good evaluation

    1              1 – Limited                1 – Limited                    1 – Limited

    0                    0                         0                              0

Indicative content:
AO2 (matrix analysis):
• Tesla-Mart: If BigBasket plays High, TM gets £12m (High) vs £6m (Low) → High dominates. If
BigBasket plays Low, TM gets £25m (High) vs £18m (Low) → High dominates. High Advertising is a
dominant strategy.
• BigBasket: Similarly, High Advertising dominates (10 > 4 and 22 > 15).
• Nash equilibrium: (High, High) → profits (£12m, £10m), total £22m.
• Collusive outcome (Low, Low): profits (£18m, £15m), total £33m.
• Both firms are £6m and £5m worse off at the Nash equilibrium than under collusion – classic
Prisoner's Dilemma.
AO3 (case for collusion):
• Collusion on reduced advertising raises joint profits by £11m (£33m vs £22m), implying wasted
expenditure on mutually cancelling advertising.
• Avoids 'advertising war' that benefits no one.
• Resources saved could be invested in R&D, lower prices, better products.
AO4 (against collusion / other considerations):
• Incentive to defect: each firm knows that if the other cooperates (Low), they can earn £25m / £22m
by defecting to High. Collusion is unstable.
• Illegal under UK and EU competition law (Cartels are enforced by the CMA, with fines up to 10% of
global turnover).
• Consumer welfare losses: reduced information, potentially higher prices, fewer choices.
• In a repeated (infinite) game, tit-for-tat strategies could sustain cooperation without explicit collusion
– tacit collusion.
• Contestable markets theory: if new entrants are possible, collusive prices attract entry, eroding
profits.
• Advertising may create genuine consumer benefit (information about price/quality) – not all
advertising is purely combative.
• Allow any other sensible point.
26. With reference to the data above, consider the extent to which increased government
spending on the NHS is the best way to correct market failure in healthcare. (8 marks)

  Band              AO2 (3)                    AO3 (2)                       AO4 (3)

    3      3 – Excellent application                             3 – Excellent evaluation
           Full use of numerical data                            Considers alternative policies,
           (budget, waiting lists,                               government failure, crowding
           private spending) AND                                 out, opportunity cost. Strong
           textual data (ageing,                                 use of theory.
           obesity).

    2         2 – Good application        2 – Good analysis            2 – Good evaluation

    1              1 – Limited               1 – Limited                   1 – Limited

    0                  0                          0                             0

Indicative content:
AO2:
• NHS budget has risen ~55% nominal (£123bn→£190bn) but only ~1.5% real per year, below 3.7%
historical average.
• Pressures are intensifying: waiting lists trebled to 7 million, waiting times doubled to 14 weeks.
• 50% rise in private healthcare spending suggests under-provision is pushing demand into the
market.
• Ageing (18% aged 65+) raises long-term demand regardless of policy.
AO3 (for increased spending):
• Healthcare is a merit good with positive externalities (herd immunity, productive workforce).
• Information asymmetry: patients cannot accurately assess healthcare quality/quantity they need,
leading to under-consumption in a free market.
• Free market would exclude low-income groups → equity concerns.
• Extra funding addresses capacity constraints (more doctors, equipment, beds), directly reducing
waiting times.
AO4 (evaluation):
• Simply 'throwing money' at the NHS risks government failure – money may not reach front-line care
(management overhead, inefficient procurement).
• Alternative policies: public-health interventions (obesity, smoking, exercise) may reduce demand
more cost-effectively – upstream prevention.
• Workforce shortages limit the productivity of extra spending – need for training, migration policy.
• Private sector partnerships or insurance-based systems (e.g. Germany) may deliver better
outcomes.
• Opportunity cost: every £1 on NHS is £1 less for education, defence, infrastructure.
• Tax increases to fund more spending may have disincentive effects.
• Structural reform (integration of health and social care, digital transformation) may boost productivity
without huge extra spending.
• Allow any other sensible point.
27. Discuss the extent to which openness to trade and foreign investment is the most effective
driver of economic development. (8 marks)

  Band              AO2 (3)                   AO3 (2)                      AO4 (3)

    3      3 – Excellent                                        3 – Excellent
           All columns referenced;                              Strong counter using theory
           specific comparisons                                 (Rodrik, institutional
           between countries made.                              economics, Washington
                                                                Consensus critique).

    2               2 – Good                 2 – Good                     2 – Good

    1             1 – Limited               1 – Limited                   1 – Limited

    0                  0                         0                            0

Indicative content:
AO2:
• Strong correlation between openness and HDI: Singapore (320% openness, HDI 0.938) vs North
Korea (~5%, est. HDI <0.5).
• Vietnam: very open (210%), high FDI (6.3%), grew 6.2% p.a., HDI rose significantly to 0.704.
• But China grew fastest (8.9%) with only moderate openness (38%) and low FDI/GDP (1.8%) –
suggests domestic factors also crucial.
• South Korea: relatively closed to FDI (1.2%) but high HDI (0.925) – state-led development model.
• Nigeria: similar openness to China (31%) but much slower growth and lower HDI – institutional
factors matter.
AO3 (for openness):
• Comparative advantage: trade allows specialisation and raises real incomes.
• FDI brings capital, technology transfer, management know-how and access to export markets.
• Competition from imports disciplines domestic firms → productivity gains.
• Access to larger markets allows EoS in production.
• Integration into global value chains (Vietnam, Singapore) generates jobs and skills.
AO4 (evaluation):
• China and South Korea show that strategic/managed openness (not laissez-faire) may be superior.
• Openness without strong institutions (Nigeria) fails to deliver development – 'resource curse' and
corruption absorb gains.
• FDI can create dependency on MNCs, with profits repatriated and limited technology transfer.
• Import competition may destroy infant industries → infant-industry argument for temporary
protection.
• Inequality can rise: Singapore (Gini 0.46) and China (0.47) both high despite growth.
• Vulnerability to external shocks (2008 GFC, Covid-19) increases with openness.
• Education, infrastructure, rule of law may be more fundamental drivers (Rodrik).
• North Korea's extreme closure is clearly counterproductive – so some openness is beneficial, but
the optimal degree and sequencing matters.
• Allow any other sensible point.
